results.possibleIdentity: Stan VanDerBeek (1927–1984) was a pioneering American multimedia artist, experimental filmmaker, and art theoretician renowned for his early integration of technology into creative practice. Active from the late 1950s through the early 1980s, he is widely credited with coining the term "expanded cinema." He was a central figure in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement, which facilitated his landmark collaboration with programmer Ken Knowlton at Bell Laboratories. Between 1966 and 1971, they produced the "Poemfield" series, which represents some of the earliest and most significant examples of computer-generated animation. VanDerBeek's most famous architectural project was the "Movie-Drome" (1963–1965) in Stony Point, New York—a geodesic dome built from a grain silo where he presented immersive, multi-projector film experiences he described as "newsreels of dreams." In the early 1970s, he served as the first artist-in-residence at MIT’s Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS), where he explored telecommunications art through fax-transmitted murals like "Panels for the Walls of the World" and interactive television broadcasts such as "Violence Sonata" (1970). His early work at Black Mountain College brought him into contact with influential figures like Buckminster Fuller, John Cage, and Merce Cunningham, shaping his lifelong interest in non-linear communication and the utopian possibilities of technology.

results.possibleIdentity: Everett Aison is an American artist, graphic designer, filmmaker, and educator who was significantly active during the late 1960s and early 1970s. He is best known for creating iconic, minimalist movie posters for prominent foreign films, including the three-color silkscreened poster for Akira Kurosawa’s 'Yojimbo' (1961), as well as posters for Roman Polanski’s 'Knife in the Water' and Louis Malle’s 'Zazie dans le métro'. In 1964–1965, he co-founded the film school at the School of Visual Arts (SVA) in New York City, which he chaired and led for nearly 40 years. Aison's work extended into filmmaking; his short film 'So Much in Common' was notably screened alongside 'Five Easy Pieces' in the early 1970s. He has been associated with the avant-garde and media experimentation scene in New York, appearing in historical contact lists and archives alongside members of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) and the Alternate Media Center, particularly in the context of community video and public access television initiatives. Additionally, he is a writer and illustrator, having authored the 2006 novel 'Artrage' and illustrated the children’s books 'Arthur' (written by Rhoda Levine) and 'The American Movie'.

results.possibleIdentity: Josiah Edgerly (also known as Josiah W. Edgerly) was an artist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s. He was an alumnus of the prestigious Skowhegan School of Painting and Sculpture, where he attended the residency program in 1961. Edgerly was associated with the New York avant-garde scene and the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) community. He was a longtime collaborator and associate of Robert Whitman, one of the four co-founders of E.A.T. Evidence of his professional and personal connection to Whitman is found in records of the MDI Biological Laboratory, where he and his partner Vivian Rea are listed as donors and community members alongside Robert T. Whitman. Edgerly's active period as an artist corresponds with the formative years of E.A.T., during which the organization promoted interdisciplinary projects between artists and engineers. Recent records from 2024 indicate that he has passed away.
